title: Debounce vs throttle — implement debounce
answer:
**Core concept (TL;DR).** *Debouncing* collapses a burst of rapid calls into a **single** call that runs only **after the activity has paused** for a set delay. Every new call resets the timer — so the function fires once, on the trailing edge, when things go quiet.
**How it works.** A debounced wrapper keeps a timer id in a closure. Each call **clears** the previous timer and starts a new one; only when calls stop long enough for a timer to survive does the real function run. This is ideal for "act when the user finishes" moments: search-as-you-type, validating an input, or saving a draft. ### Debounce vs throttle | | Debounce | Throttle | | --- | --- | --- | | Fires | once, after calls stop | at most once per interval | | Resets timer on each call | yes | no | | Best for | search box, autosave, resize-settled | scroll, drag, mousemove, rate-limit | | Mental model | "wait until they're done" | "no more than once every N ms" | > **Interview tip:** Be ready to implement it in ~6 lines and to add a cancel() method (clears the pending timer). Interviewers often follow up by asking how throttle differs — answer with the table above.

examples.label: A 6-line debounce

title: Describe the concept of 'scope' in JavaScript.
answer:
**Core concept (TL;DR).** *Scope* is the region of a program where a given name is accessible. JavaScript has three flavours: **global** (visible everywhere), **function** (visible anywhere inside the function — how var behaves), and **block** (visible only inside { } — how let/const behave). Scopes nest, and inner scopes can read outward.
**How it works.** A name is resolved by looking in the current scope first, then each enclosing scope, out to global — that search path is the *scope chain*. The crucial split: var ignores blocks and attaches to the nearest **function** (or global), so a var declared inside an if leaks out of it; let and const are confined to the **block**. Each function call also creates a fresh scope, which is what lets closures capture private state. ### The three scopes | Scope | Created by | Holds | | --- | --- | --- | | Global | the top level | var at top level, globalThis props | | Function | any function call | var, params, inner functions | | Block | a { } block | let, const | > **Interview tip:** The headline contrast is "var is function-scoped; let/const are block-scoped." Be ready to show that a var inside an if block is still visible after the block, whereas a let there is not.

examples.label: Function scope vs block scope

title: Explain 'this' keyword in JavaScript.
answer:
**Core concept (TL;DR).** this is **not** decided by where a function is written — it is decided by **how the function is called** (the call-site). Four rules resolve it, checked from highest precedence down; arrow functions opt out entirely and inherit this from their surrounding scope.
**How it works.** At every call the engine asks, in order: was it called with new? with .call/.apply/.bind? as a method on an object? otherwise it falls back to the default (undefined in strict mode, the global object otherwise). An arrow function has no this of its own, so it transparently uses the this of the scope it was created in — which is exactly why arrows are the fix for "lost this" inside callbacks. ### Resolving the binding | Call form | this resolves to | | --- | --- | | new Counter() | the freshly created instance | | fn.call(obj) / fn.bind(obj) | obj (explicit) | | user.greet() | user (the receiver) | | const g = user.greet; g() | undefined — receiver is lost | | arrow inside a method | the method's this (lexical) | > **Interview tip:** The most common bug is passing a method as a callback (setTimeout(user.greet)) — the receiver is dropped and this becomes undefined. Fix it with .bind(user) or by wrapping in an arrow.

examples.label: How the call-site changes 'this'

title: Explain event delegation.
answer:
**Core Concept (TL;DR)** Event delegation is a performance optimization pattern where a single event listener is attached to a parent container to manage events fired by multiple child elements, leveraging DOM event bubbling.
**Under-the-Hood Mechanics** 1. **Bubble Phase Propagation**: When an event occurs on a child node, it bubbles up through its ancestors in the DOM tree. 2. **Unified Handling**: By listening to the container, we intercept the event on its way up. 3. **Dynamic Filtering**: Inside the handler, we inspect `event.target` (the actual clicked node) to determine if it matches the child nodes we care about, utilizing APIs like `element.matches()`. **Tricky Code Scenarios & Gotchas** - **Nested Child Elements**: If a button contains a nested span or icon, `event.target` will refer to the span/icon instead of the button. ```javascript // Fix: Use event.target.closest('button') to find the target element context! container.addEventListener("click", (e) => { const button = e.target.closest("button"); if (button && container.contains(button)) { console.log("Button clicked:", button.id); } }); ``` - **Non-Bubbling Events**: Some DOM events (like `focus`, `blur`, `mouseenter`, `mouseleave`) do not bubble. To delegate them, you must catch them in the **Capturing Phase** by setting `useCapture = true` in the event listener. **Architectural Rationale** Event delegation saves memory by replacing thousands of individual element listeners with a single listener. It also supports dynamic DOM manipulation, automatically handling nodes added to the DOM after mounting without requiring new listeners. **Pro-Level Interview Tip** Explain how to handle nested elements using `closest()` and resolve delegation for non-bubbling events (like `focus`) by capturing them instead.

examples.label: one listener for a whole list (DOM)

title: Explain how prototypal inheritance differs from classical inheritance.
answer:
**Core concept (TL;DR).** In *classical* inheritance (Java, C++, C#) classes are blueprints, and objects are instances stamped out from them at compile time. In *prototypal* inheritance (JavaScript) there are no blueprints — objects inherit **directly from other objects** through a live [[Prototype]] link, resolved dynamically at runtime.
**How it works.** Classical languages copy a class's structure into each instance at definition time; the hierarchy is rigid. JavaScript instead links objects: a lookup that misses on an object walks up its prototype chain at runtime, so you can even change behaviour by swapping a prototype. The class keyword (ES2015) *looks* classical but is pure **syntactic sugar** — it still wires up prototype links underneath, with no real classes involved. ### Side by side | | Classical | Prototypal (JS) | | --- | --- | --- | | Inherit from | classes (blueprints) | live objects | | Resolved | compile time | runtime (dynamic) | | Methods | copied per class | shared on the prototype | | Flexibility | rigid hierarchy | reshape at runtime | | Keyword | real class | class = sugar over prototypes | > **Interview tip:** The line interviewers want to hear: "JavaScript's class is syntactic sugar over prototypes — there are no real classes; method lookup is a dynamic walk up the prototype chain." Mention Object.create as the most direct expression of object-to-object inheritance.

examples.label: class is sugar; Object.create is the raw form

title: Explain prototypal inheritance.
answer:
**Core concept (TL;DR).** In JavaScript objects inherit directly from other **objects**. Every object has a hidden link ([[Prototype]], exposed as __proto__) to another object. When you read a property that an object doesn't own, the engine walks **up this prototype chain** until it finds the property or reaches null.
**How it works.** Methods are stored **once** on the prototype and shared by every instance, instead of being copied onto each object — that is the memory win. class syntax (ES2015) is sugar over exactly this: class Dog extends Animal still wires up prototype links under the hood. You can build the same relationship manually with Object.create(proto) or a constructor function plus Constructor.prototype. ### Prototypal vs classical | | Prototypal (JS) | Classical (Java/C++) | | --- | --- | --- | | Inherit from | live objects | classes (blueprints) | | Method storage | shared on the prototype | copied per class definition | | Lookup | dynamic walk up the chain | fixed at compile time | | Set up with | Object.create, class, ctor fn | class keyword only | > **Interview tip:** Note that class in JS is *syntactic sugar* over prototypes — there are no real classes underneath. Mention that own properties shadow inherited ones, and hasOwnProperty distinguishes the two.

examples.label: Shared methods via the prototype chain

title: Explain the event loop, call stack, and task queues.
answer:
**Core Concept (TL;DR)** The Event Loop is the asynchronous scheduler of the JavaScript runtime. It monitors the Call Stack and task queues, dequeuing microtasks and macrotasks sequentially to enable concurrent, non-blocking I/O execution on a single thread.
**Under-the-Hood Mechanics** JavaScript executes on a single call stack. To perform non-blocking actions, tasks are offloaded to browser Web APIs (or Node.js thread pools). When asynchronous actions complete, their callbacks enter the task queues: 1. **Call Stack**: Executes synchronous contexts frame-by-frame. 2. **Microtask Queue**: Drains completely after the Call Stack is empty. 3. **Macrotask Queue**: Runs exactly one task per event loop cycle. 4. **Layout & Paint**: Re-renders the display if the frame timer (typically 16.7ms) is ready. **Tricky Code Scenarios & Gotchas** - **Starving the Loop**: If a microtask continuously schedules more microtasks, the event loop remains trapped in the microtask phase, stalling macrotasks (like timers) and blocking rendering, causing the browser tab to freeze. - **rAF vs SetTimeout**: `requestAnimationFrame` callbacks execute as part of the rendering pipeline, making them smoother for visual layouts compared to `setTimeout` macrotasks. **Architectural Rationale** The event loop design enables high-concurrency servers (Node.js) and responsive browser UIs without threading overhead, race conditions, or context-switching costs. **Pro-Level Interview Tip** Describe the loop lifecycle sequentially (Stack -> Microtasks -> Rendering -> Macrotask). Use terms like "Starving the event loop" and "Frame rendering budget" to show production-level architectural expertise.

examples.label: stack, microtask, macrotask

title: How do getters and setters work?
answer:
**Core concept (TL;DR).** Getters and setters are **accessor properties**: a get function runs when the property is **read**, and a set function runs when it's **assigned**. From the outside they look like a normal property (obj.fullName), but behind the scenes they compute, validate, or react — no parentheses needed.
**How it works.** You define them with get/set keywords in object literals and classes, or via Object.defineProperty. A getter takes no args and returns the value; a setter takes the assigned value and usually stores it in a "backing" field. They enable computed properties (derive fullName from parts), validation on write, lazy computation, and read-only properties (a getter with no setter). The trade-off: a property access can now hide expensive work or side effects, so keep them cheap and predictable. ### Accessor vs data property | | Data property | Accessor (get/set) | | --- | --- | --- | | Stores a value | yes | no (computes) | | Runs code on read | no | yes (get) | | Runs code on write | no | yes (set) | | Read-only | writable: false | omit the set | > **Interview tip:** Note that a getter without a setter makes a clean **read-only/computed** property, and that setters are the idiomatic place for **validation** on assignment. Caution: they can disguise costly work behind innocent-looking property access.

examples.label: Computed property + validation

title: How do iterators and the iterable protocol work?
answer:
**Core concept (TL;DR).** An object is **iterable** if it has a [Symbol.iterator]() method that returns an **iterator**. An iterator is any object with a next() method returning { value, done }. Together these two protocols are what for…of, spread, and destructuring use to walk a sequence.
**How it works.** for…of calls [Symbol.iterator]() once to get an iterator, then calls next() repeatedly, using each value until done is true. Arrays, strings, Map, Set, and arguments are built-in iterables. You can make any object iterable by implementing the method yourself — and **generators** (function*) produce iterator objects automatically, which is why they're the easy way to author custom iteration. ### The two protocols | Protocol | Requirement | | --- | --- | | Iterable | a [Symbol.iterator]() method | | Iterator | a next() → { value, done } | | Consumed by | for…of, spread ..., destructuring | | Easiest to author with | a generator (function* + yield) | > **Interview tip:** Point out that a generator object is **both** an iterator *and* iterable (its [Symbol.iterator] returns itself), so you can for…of over a generator directly. That's the cleanest answer when asked to make something iterable.

examples.label: A hand-written iterable range

title: How do you convert a callback-based function to a Promise?
answer:
**Core concept (TL;DR).** Wrap the callback API in a new Promise: call the original function inside the executor, and translate its callback into resolve / reject. For Node's (err, data) convention, reject on err and resolve with data. This is exactly what util.promisify automates.
**How it works.** The Promise executor runs synchronously; you invoke the callback-style function inside it and settle the promise from within the callback. Once wrapped, the function composes with .then/.catch and async/await, escaping "callback hell." Resolve a promise once — later resolve/reject calls are ignored. ### Checklist when promisifying | Step | Why | | --- | --- | | return new Promise((resolve, reject) => …) | hand back a thenable | | call the original fn inside | preserve its behaviour | | reject(err) on failure | so .catch/try works | | resolve(data) on success | hand the value downstream | > **Interview tip:** Mention util.promisify (Node) and that many modern APIs (fetch, fs.promises) are already promise-based. A good follow-up is wrapping setTimeout into a delay(ms) helper — the canonical one-liner promisify.

examples.label: Promisify a Node-style callback

title: How do you deep clone an object in JavaScript?
answer:
**Core concept (TL;DR).** A *deep* clone copies an object **and all nested values**, so the copy shares no references with the original (mutating one never affects the other). The modern built-in is structuredClone(); the old trick JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(x)) works but is lossy; spread/Object.assign are only **shallow**.
**How it works.** structuredClone(value) is the right default: it recursively copies nested objects and supports Date, Map, Set, typed arrays, and **circular references** — but not functions or DOM nodes (it throws on those). JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(x)) is a handy one-liner but silently drops functions/undefined/symbols, turns Dates into strings, and throws on cycles. Spread and Object.assign copy only the first level, so nested objects remain shared. ### Cloning options | Technique | Depth / notes | | --- | --- | | {...o} / Object.assign | shallow only | | JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(o)) | deep but lossy, no cycles | | structuredClone(o) | deep, Dates/Maps/cycles ✓ (no fns) | | library (Lodash cloneDeep) | deep, configurable | > **Interview tip:** Recommend structuredClone as the modern default and explain the JSON method's losses (functions/undefined/Dates) and its failure on circular references. Remember spread is **shallow**.

examples.label: Shallow vs deep

title: How do you handle asynchronous operations in JavaScript?
answer:
**Core concept (TL;DR).** There are three generations of async handling, each fixing the last one's pain: **callbacks** (a function to run when work finishes), **Promises** (a chainable object representing a future value), and **async/await** (syntax that lets you write Promise-based code in a flat, synchronous-looking style). Modern code defaults to async/await.
**How it works.** A **Promise** is in one of three states — *pending*, then settled as *fulfilled* or *rejected* — and you react with .then/.catch/.finally. async/await is built directly on Promises: await pauses the async function until the Promise settles (without blocking the thread), and errors surface through ordinary try/catch. To run independent tasks concurrently, combine them with Promise.all (all succeed), Promise.allSettled (collect every outcome), or Promise.race. ### Combinators worth naming | Helper | Resolves when | Use for | | --- | --- | --- | | Promise.all | all fulfil (rejects fast) | parallel "need everything" | | Promise.allSettled | all settle | collect successes + failures | | Promise.race | first settles | timeouts, fastest-wins | | Promise.any | first fulfils | first success, ignore errors | > **Interview tip:** Emphasise that await in a loop runs **sequentially** — if the tasks are independent, gather the promises and await Promise.all(...) instead for big speedups. Also: always handle rejections, or you'll get an unhandled-rejection warning.

examples.label: Promise chaining vs async/await + Promise.all

title: How do you implement a throttle function?
answer:
**Core concept (TL;DR).** *Throttling* guarantees a function runs **at most once per time window**, no matter how often it is called. Unlike debounce (which waits for silence), throttle lets calls through on a **fixed cadence** — perfect for high-frequency events you still want to react to *while* they happen.
**How it works.** A throttle remembers the timestamp (or a "cooling down" flag) of the last allowed call. A new call is permitted only if enough time has elapsed since then; otherwise it is dropped (or, in fancier versions, scheduled as a single trailing call). This keeps expensive handlers — scroll position math, drag updates, analytics pings — running smoothly instead of firing hundreds of times a second. ### When to reach for which | Scenario | Use | | --- | --- | | Search-as-you-type, autosave | debounce | | Scroll / resize position tracking | throttle | | Drag-and-drop move handler | throttle | | Button double-click guard | throttle (leading) | > **Interview tip:** Mention "leading vs trailing" edges — a leading throttle fires immediately then ignores for the window; a trailing one also fires once at the window's end so the final state isn't lost.

examples.label: Leading-edge throttle

title: How do you make an AJAX request?
answer:
**Core concept (TL;DR).** AJAX (Asynchronous JavaScript And XML) is the technique of exchanging data with a server **in the background** and updating the page without a full reload. The original API is XMLHttpRequest (XHR); the modern, Promise-based replacement is fetch, and libraries like Axios wrap either.
**How it works.** The page issues an HTTP request from JavaScript and, when the response arrives, updates only the affected part of the UI — no navigation. With fetch you call fetch(url), await the Response, then parse the body (.json()). XHR is the older, event-driven, callback-based equivalent. Despite the "XML" in the name, the payload today is almost always **JSON**. ### AJAX options | Approach | Style | | --- | --- | | fetch | promise-based, built-in (modern default) | | XMLHttpRequest | callback/event-based (legacy) | | Axios / ky | library wrappers, nicer ergonomics | > **Interview tip:** Say AJAX is a *technique*, not an API — today it's done with fetch/async-await. Mention that "XML" is historical; JSON is the norm.

examples.label: fetch (modern) vs XMLHttpRequest (legacy)

title: How does `try...catch` work in JavaScript?
answer:
**Core concept (TL;DR).** try wraps code that might throw; if anything inside throws, control jumps to catch with the error; an optional finally block runs **either way** (success or failure). It only catches **synchronous** throws — and rejected promises you await — not errors thrown later in async callbacks.
**How it works.** When code in try throws (manually with throw, or from a built-in like JSON.parse), the engine stops and runs catch with the thrown value (usually an Error with name/message/stack). finally always executes — ideal for cleanup. With async/await, a rejected awaited promise behaves like a sync throw, so you can wrap await in try...catch. But a throw inside a setTimeout/event callback runs on a later turn and escapes the original try — handle those where they actually run. ### Pieces & limits | Part | Role | | --- | --- | | try | code that may throw | | catch (e) | runs on a thrown error | | finally | always runs (cleanup) | | catches | sync throws + awaited rejections | | misses | throws in async callbacks/timers | > **Interview tip:** Two depth points: finally runs even if try/catch returns; and try...catch can't catch async-callback errors — use it around await, or attach .catch() to the promise.

examples.label: Sync throw, finally, and awaited rejection

title: How does instanceof work?
answer:
**Core concept (TL;DR).** obj instanceof Ctor returns true if Ctor.prototype appears **anywhere in obj's prototype chain**. It tests the inheritance relationship, not a "type" — so it works for objects/instances but not for primitives.
**How it works.** The engine starts at Object.getPrototypeOf(obj) and follows [[Prototype]] links, checking each against Ctor.prototype until it matches (true) or hits null (false). Two gotchas: it returns false for **primitives** ("hi" instanceof String is false), and it can fail **across realms** (an array from another iframe isn't instanceof your Array) — which is why Array.isArray() exists. ### instanceof vs the alternatives | Tool | Best for | | --- | --- | | instanceof | custom class / inheritance checks | | typeof | primitives ("string", "number"…) | | Array.isArray() | arrays (realm-safe) | | Object.prototype.toString.call() | precise built-in tag | > **Interview tip:** Mention the cross-realm pitfall and Array.isArray. Bonus depth: instanceof is customizable via the Symbol.hasInstance well-known symbol.

examples.label: Chain checks, primitives, and arrays

title: How does the fetch API work?
answer:
**Core concept (TL;DR).** fetch(url, options) returns a **Promise** that resolves to a Response object. You read the body with another promise-returning method (.json(), .text(), .blob()). The big gotcha: fetch only **rejects** on a network failure — an HTTP 404/500 still *resolves*, so you must check response.ok yourself.
**How it works.** A basic GET is const res = await fetch(url); const data = await res.json();. For other methods you pass options: method, headers, and a body (often JSON.stringify(...)). Reading the body is itself async because the response can stream. Because non-2xx responses resolve normally, robust code checks if (!res.ok) throw .... Other notes: fetch doesn't send cookies cross-origin unless credentials: "include", and you cancel it with an AbortController. ### fetch essentials | Step / option | Detail | | --- | --- | | await fetch(url) | resolves to a Response | | res.ok / res.status | check before trusting | | await res.json() | parse the body (a promise) | | method/headers/body | configure POST/PUT | | AbortController | cancel an in-flight request | > **Interview tip:** The classic gotcha to volunteer: fetch does **not** reject on 4xx/5xx — always check res.ok. Add: body parsing is async, and you need AbortController for cancellation/timeouts.

examples.label: GET, error-check, and POST JSON

title: What are Symbols and what are they used for?
answer:
**Core concept (TL;DR).** Symbol is a primitive type whose every value is **guaranteed unique** — Symbol("x") !== Symbol("x"). Their main job is **collision-free object keys**: a symbol property never clashes with a string key, even one with the same description.
**How it works.** Symbols are created with Symbol(desc) (the description is just a label for debugging). As keys they're skipped by for…in, Object.keys, and JSON.stringify, making them ideal for metadata you don't want to leak. JavaScript also ships **well-known symbols** that let you hook into language behaviour — Symbol.iterator makes an object iterable, Symbol.asyncIterator, Symbol.hasInstance, and more. ### Uses for symbols | Use | Why a symbol | | --- | --- | | Unique object keys | never clash with other keys | | "Hidden" metadata | invisible to Object.keys / JSON | | Well-known hooks | Symbol.iterator etc. customize behaviour | | Global registry | Symbol.for("k") shares one across the app | > **Interview tip:** Distinguish Symbol() (always unique) from Symbol.for("k") (looked up in a global registry, so the same key returns the same symbol). And note symbol keys aren't truly private — Object.getOwnPropertySymbols can still find them.

examples.label: Uniqueness and collision-free keys

title: What are WeakMap and WeakSet?
answer:
**Core concept (TL;DR).** WeakMap and WeakSet are collections that hold their keys/values **weakly**: entries reference objects without preventing them from being garbage-collected. When the only reference left to an object is inside a WeakMap/WeakSet, it can be reclaimed automatically — preventing memory leaks.
**How it works.** Because the references are weak, the engine can collect an entry as soon as its key object is unreachable elsewhere. That forces two restrictions: keys (WeakMap) / values (WeakSet) **must be objects**, and the collection is **not iterable** and has no .size (you can't enumerate something that might vanish mid-iteration). Classic uses: attaching **private data** or **metadata** to objects (e.g. DOM nodes) and **caches** keyed by object — all without leaking memory when those objects go away. ### WeakMap/WeakSet vs Map/Set | | Map/Set | WeakMap/WeakSet | | --- | --- | --- | | Keys/values | any | objects only | | References | strong | weak (GC-able) | | Iterable / .size | yes | no | | Best for | general collections | per-object metadata, caches | > **Interview tip:** The headline: WeakMap lets you associate data with an object **without keeping it alive**, so it's leak-free caching/private-state. Explain that "not iterable / no size" is a *consequence* of weak references — the contents can disappear non-deterministically.

examples.label: Object-keyed, non-iterable metadata store

title: What are `let`, `const`, and `var`? What are their differences?
answer:
**Core concept (TL;DR).** All three declare variables, but differ on **scope**, **hoisting/initialization**, and **reassignment**. var is function-scoped and hoisted as undefined; let and const are block-scoped and sit in a *temporal dead zone* until their declaration; const additionally forbids reassignment of the binding.
**How it works.** All declarations are "hoisted," but only var is **initialized** to undefined at the top of its function — so reading it early is harmless but useless. let/const are hoisted *without* initialization, so reading them before their line throws a ReferenceError (the TDZ). const locks the **binding**, not the value: a const object can still have its properties mutated. ### Decision table | | var | let | const | | --- | --- | --- | --- | | Scope | function | block | block | | Hoisted as | undefined | TDZ | TDZ | | Reassign | yes | yes | no | | Redeclare in scope | yes | no | no | > **Interview tip:** Modern guidance: const by default, let only when you must reassign, var essentially never. Be ready to explain that const prevents *rebinding*, not *mutation* — const arr = []; arr.push(1) is perfectly legal.

examples.label: Hoisting, TDZ, and const mutation

title: What are async iterators and for await...of?
answer:
**Core concept (TL;DR).** An *async iterator* is the asynchronous twin of a normal iterator: its next() returns a **Promise** of { value, done } instead of a plain object. An object is *async iterable* via [Symbol.asyncIterator](), and for await…of is the loop that consumes it — awaiting each step before moving on.
**How it works.** Where a sync iterator must have all its data ready, an async iterator can await between values — so each item can arrive from the network or disk. async function* generators are the easy way to author one: every yield can follow an await. for await…of only works inside an async function, and it processes items **sequentially** (it awaits each before the next). ### Sync vs async iteration | | Sync iterator | Async iterator | | --- | --- | --- | | Hook | [Symbol.iterator] | [Symbol.asyncIterator] | | next() returns | { value, done } | Promise<{ value, done }> | | Consumed by | for…of | for await…of | | Authored with | function* | async function* | > **Interview tip:** Note that for await…of is *sequential* — to run many async tasks in parallel you'd use Promise.all instead. Real-world fit: paginating an API, or reading a Node stream (which is async-iterable out of the box).

examples.label: An async generator + for await…of
